title: 31_direct_state_mutation.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Role Badge Never Updates After Clicking Promote

An admin panel has a button that promotes a user from `viewer` to `admin`. Clicking 'Promote to Admin' runs `promoteToAdmin()` — but the role shown in the UI never changes.

The developer checks `console.log(user.role)` after clicking and sees `'admin'` — so the mutation happened. But the component never re-rendered.

Fix the state update so React detects the change and re-renders the badge.
traceback:
User clicks 'Promote to Admin'.
  console.log(user.role) → 'admin'   (mutation ran)
  UI still shows: Role: viewer       (no re-render)

React compares the previous state reference with the new one.
`setUser(user)` passes the same object — React shallow-compares
references, sees no change, and bails out of the re-render.
correctOutput:
Click 'Promote to Admin':
  UI updates to show: Role: admin

React re-renders because setUser received a NEW object,
not the same reference with a mutated property.

options.label: Spread the existing object into a new one with the updated role
options.resultLog:
Click 'Promote to Admin':
  UI updates immediately → Role: admin

`{ ...user, role: 'admin' }` creates a NEW object.
React receives a different reference and schedules a re-render.
options.rationale: React performs a shallow reference comparison on state. If `setUser` receives the same object reference it had before, React treats the state as unchanged and skips the re-render. `{ ...user, role: 'admin' }` creates a brand-new object with the updated property — React sees a different reference and re-renders the component.

options.label: Mutate first, then force a re-render by calling `setUser({})`
options.resultLog:
Click 'Promote to Admin':
  UI updates — but now `user` is `{}` (empty object).
  Name: (blank)  Role: (blank)

The `user` state is now an empty object, not the updated user.
The mutation happened but the state was replaced with `{}`.
options.rationale: Passing `{}` to `setUser` replaces the user state with an empty object. The UI re-renders, but now `user.name` and `user.role` are both `undefined`. You've clobbered the entire user state while trying to force a re-render. Always update state by creating a new object that includes all existing fields plus the changes.

options.label: Use `Object.assign(user, { role: 'admin' })` then call `setUser(user)`
options.resultLog:
Click 'Promote to Admin':
  UI may or may not update depending on React version.
  In React 18+ with batching: likely no re-render.

`Object.assign(user, ...)` mutates `user` in place — same as writing
`user.role = 'admin'` directly. `setUser(user)` still passes the
same object reference. React still sees no state change.
options.rationale: `Object.assign(target, source)` copies properties onto the `target` object — it mutates `user` in place. The object reference is unchanged. `setUser(user)` passes the same reference as before, so React skips the re-render. This is the same mutation bug as the original, just expressed differently.

hints: React compares the previous and next state with `Object.is()` (shallow reference equality). If you pass the same object reference, React concludes nothing changed and skips re-rendering. Log `user === prevUser` after `setUser(user)` to see this.

Create a new object instead of mutating: `setUser({ ...user, role: 'admin' })`. The spread operator copies all properties of `user` into a new object, then the `role` property is overwritten. React receives a different reference and re-renders.
recruiterReview: Correct. Direct state mutation is the most common React beginner mistake. The `Object.assign` distractor is the same mutation bug in disguise — it catches candidates who know the name but not the semantics. The empty-object distractor shows what happens when you force a re-render carelessly. The spread operator creating a new object is the fundamental React immutability pattern. ✅
